translation.content: "We're making history tonight," hollered the MC at the start of Nooriyah's London Boiler Room in 2022. Sat next to the decks was her baba (Arabic for father), dressed in traditional Saudi garb. He opened the one-hour performance by playing the oud, a Middle Eastern instrument similar to a lute. Surrounded by smiling faces and pumping arms, it's a picture of joy. This moment was a turning point—and not just for Nooriyah's career. Scroll through the comments on YouTube and you'll find notes of endearment, gratitude and teary appreciation, proof of how powerful it was for people to see Middle Eastern music placed at the centre of contemporary club culture. It's a central insight into Nooriyah's MO. Born in Saudi Arabia, raised in Japan and now based in the UK, her musical vision reflects her global upbringing. But her style isn't eclecticism for eclecticism's sake—she's spoken about the importance of carving out space for underrepresented voices in dance music. "How I see it is that I'm not only providing a sense of belonging through music, but also sort of bridging the gap between our differences and de-stigmatising SWANA identity," she told DJ Mag. "I want people to see that we're more similar to you than you might think we are." Her RA Mix makes that mission audible. As she explains in her own words below, it's "a trip around the world, holding eight different languages and even more sub-genres with a forward West Asian and North African twist and sprinkles of playfulness." The result is a breathless hour: 47 tracks darting between speed garage, amapiano, Jersey club, Arabic pop edits and percussion-heavy workouts from Cairo to Accra. But don't mistake pace for carelessness: RA.1002 never feels rushed. Each switch is considered, revealing a knowledge of how global dance traditions can speak to one another. All in all, it's not only a celebration of her own heritage, but an invitation to imagine dance floors unconstrained by borders. -Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? You're catching me at the tail end of festival season. I've just returned from a nonsensical three-day festival run, so I've been mostly prepping, sleeping with my mouth open on planes, jumping up and down then returning to my mum's house to steal some home-cooked meals—and repeat. In between all of this, I've been experimenting on my first EP. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? The mix was recorded on an XDJ-RX2 at my place in London while sporting the pyjama version of a jalabiya, a loose-fitting dress that my mama gave me. It allows me to feel any breeze that the London heat can afford to give me right now. The idea behind the mix is a collection of tracks that made me scrunch my face (like I've smelt something terrible) yet so far haven't made it onto a festival stage this summer. These are absolute tasty gems that maybe didn't necessarily fit in the tempo, key or story of my festival sets, but deserved a place to live together in this setting. In the usual Nooriyah palate, the mix is a trip around the world, holding eight different languages and even more sub-genres with a forward West African and North African twist and sprinkles of playfulness. Most suited for dancing your heart out at home, in the car, in the shower or while cooking. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Good sleep and family time! This festival season kicked my ass. Aside from looking forward to being cooped up in my pajamas and working on more in the studio, I'm excited for my debut India tour and second European tour coming up. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? [For] my Master's in public health, my dissertation focussed on child nutrition provision in conflict zones. This topic is near and dear to my heart, as I believe no person—let alone a child in crucial developmental years—should starve due to any reason, government or oppressor. Today, sadly, there are a few areas still facing severe child starvation and malnutrition and I wish the world and the media would pay more attention to crises in places such as Sudan and Yemen, in addition to Gaza. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? PXSSY PALACE. It's been a joy seeing the party get better and better over the years and it's all in the details. They made their own space, their own rules and they ensured that people have support on the dance floor and on their way home, if needed. They left a huge impact on me in how I learned to take care of attendees when I'm producing events. For example, I hired some of their trained safety officers for my Middle of Nowhere party. I just think they're exemplary, from both an event production and curator perspective. Another party that has [had an impact on me] and many others lately is Club Soda. It's a new alcohol-free, day party that rethinks what clubbing is and the current structure of money-making in the scene. I'm an alcohol-free person, so I'm so into these spaces and ideas.
translation.blurb: From speed garage to Arabic pop, one hour of borderless club energy from the Saudi DJ and curator.
